lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — eGenesis: the 69-edit competitor
The other major player is eGenesis, founded in 2015 by George Church and Luhan Yang — the principal investigators on the 2017 PERV-knockout paper we discussed in segment 6.
eGenesis is based in Cambridge, Massachusetts, near the Harvard and MIT campuses where Church has his lab.
The company's founding bet was different from Revivicor's.
Revivicor came up through the cloning and homologous recombination era and added CRISPR later.
eGenesis was born in the CRISPR era and built its entire pipeline around multiplexed gene editing.
Their advantage was always going to be the ability to make many edits at once.

The eGenesis pig is sometimes called the 69-edit pig, though the count depends on how you tally — some descriptions are of 59 edits, some are of 69, depending on which subset of the porcine retroviruses are counted and which transgenes are bundled together.
The order of magnitude is right: roughly seventy genetic interventions, in one animal.
Let me walk through what's in there.
Three antigen knockouts — the triple knockout, alpha-gal, Neu5Gc, Sda — the same as Revivicor.
Sixty-plus PERV inactivations — every functional copy of porcine endogenous retrovirus knocked out, descending from the 2017 Church work.

Seven human transgenes — complement regulators including CD46 and CD55, anticoagulants including thrombomodulin and CD39, and immune modulators including HLA-E, a human MHC molecule that signals "self" to certain immune cells.
The arithmetic of building this pig is staggering.
To make a fibroblast cell line with seventy edits, you cannot do them one at a time and clone in between — you would run out of time and the cells would senesce.

You have to use multiplexed CRISPR, dropping in many guide RNAs simultaneously, and accept that you will get a fraction of cells with every edit you wanted plus some unintended consequences, and then screen by deep sequencing for the rare clone that's clean.
Once you have such a clone, you do the nuclear transfer step, implant in a surrogate sow, and get piglets that carry all seventy edits.
The eGenesis pigs that went into the 2024 kidney patients were named, in some early presentations, the EGEN-2784 line.
The number isn't important — what matters is that this is the most heavily engineered animal that has ever existed and survived to clinical use.
The two companies have a friendly rivalry.
They use slightly different breeds — Revivicor uses Yucatan, eGenesis uses Yucatan as well but with some line differences.
They make different bets on the right edit set.
Revivicor leans on barrier herd management to handle PERV; eGenesis leans on edit-out.
Revivicor was first to clinical, with Bennett's heart.
eGenesis came in shortly after with the 2024 kidney wave.
Both are now running clinical trials in coordination with the FDA.
Both have institutional partners — Revivicor with the University of Maryland and the University of Alabama at Birmingham, eGenesis with Massachusetts General Hospital and NYU Langone.
The competition is healthy.

It's the kind of two-company race that, in other fields, has driven the technology forward — think of the way Genentech and Eli Lilly competed in early recombinant insulin, or the way Illumina and the original 454 competed in next-generation sequencing.
You want two contenders.
The field is better for it.
Okay.
Now we get to the human patients.
Real people.
Real surgeries.
Real outcomes.
This is where xenotransplantation stopped being a slide deck and started being history.
